items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
I have vague misgivings again...

Shpak’s missing a tape recorder,
the ambassador’s missing a medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: Core comedic effect is mock-heroic/highbrow phrasing (“терзают смутные сомнения”) applied to petty theft logic; and the second clause is elliptical and implies loss (not possession). The actual translation preserves the highbrow-ish “vague misgivings” and correctly renders the loss state via “missing,” which matches the reference warning not to translate as “has.” Slightly less theatrical than “haunt/torment,” but still works.

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation: Hello, Tsar—very pleased. Tsar—very pleased, Tsar. Very pleased—hello, Tsar.
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: The humor is the anachronistic, Soviet-formula politeness and repetitive self-introduction as “the Tsar.” The actual translation keeps the repetition and awkward politeness, but it drops the key self-identifying “I’m the Tsar” framing and turns it into direct address (“Hello, Tsar”), which weakens the joke (Bunsha introducing himself like at a reception) and slightly shifts the pragmatic function.

items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Identical in meaning and effect; preserves the pompous authoritarian register and comic overreach. No issues.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me, old woman—I am in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: Matches the reference essentially verbatim and keeps the archaic/high-register melancholy that clashes with the mundane setting. No issues.

items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Meaning is preserved (being walled/bricked in; demons as agents). However, the English is clipped and slightly unidiomatic (“Bricked in!” without subject), losing some of the melodramatic lament quality of the original/reference. Still comprehensible and funny enough in context.

items.translation: That’s what the life-giving cross can do!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Accurate and keeps the religious framing. Slightly less mock-solemn than “Behold the power…”, but still conveys the same comic conviction in a Soviet/modern-tech context.

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: The line is a punchy MC-style command; the reference aims to capture the “open floor”/announcement vibe rather than a bare imperative. “Everybody dance!” is understandable but sounds like non-native/telegraphic English and loses the idiomatic ‘announcement’ feel (and some of the comic ‘host’ persona).

items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Captures the upbeat satisfaction at a fortuitous arrival. It slightly shifts from “I dropped in at the right time” to a broader “lucky day,” but the pragmatic function (cheerful criminal glee) remains and plays well for an English audience.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: Identical to the reference and follows the key decision not to call eggplant spread “caviar.” No issues.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation:
Everything’s gone: three tape recorders,
three movie cameras,

three domestic cigarette cases,
a suede jacket... three.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: Preserves the escalating inventory and the comic trailing self-correction on the suede jacket count. “Domestic cigarette cases” is slightly awkward but conveys “отечественных.” Overall the joke lands.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation:
And they’ll cure you...
and they’ll cure you too...
and I’ll be cured!
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: First two clauses match the resigned, sing-song repetition. The last clause shifts from “they’ll cure me” to “I’ll be cured,” changing the agent and slightly weakening the shared, deadpan acceptance of psychiatric ‘treatment’ as something done to all of them. Still understandable and close in effect.

summary.interpretation: 2.55 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.51 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
